title: Bit Manipulation
description: Working directly with a number's binary representation using &, |, ^, ~, <<, >> -- a completely different toolkit from arrays, trees, or DP.
objectives: Use AND, OR, XOR, NOT, and shift operators on integers
Check, set, and clear a specific bit
Use XOR's self-canceling property to find a single non-duplicate in an array

sections.text: Every int in Java (Data Types) is stored as 32 bits internally. Bit manipulation operates DIRECTLY on those bits, using operators we haven't covered since a brief mention in the Operators lesson.

sections.title: Bitwise operators
rows: Shifts bits left, filling with 0 -- equivalent to multiplying by 2 per shift
Shifts bits right -- equivalent to dividing by 2 per shift (rounding toward negative infinity)

sections.text: 2. Checking, Setting, and Clearing a Specific Bit

1 << position creates a MASK with only that one bit set -- e.g. 1 << 1 is 0010. ANDing with this mask (isBitSet) isolates just that bit; ORing with it (setBit) forces that bit to 1 without disturbing others; ANDing with its NOT (clearBit) forces that bit to 0 without disturbing others.

sections.title: Why XOR is uniquely useful
points: x ^ x = 0 -- any number XORed with itself cancels to zero
x ^ 0 = x -- XORing with zero changes nothing
XOR is commutative and associative -- order doesn't matter, so a ^ b ^ a always simplifies to b, regardless of grouping

sections.text: 4. Classic Application: Find the Single Non-Duplicate

sections.title: Tracing findSingle({4, 1, 2, 1, 2})
steps: XOR with 1: result=7^1=6 -- this cancels the FIRST 1's effect, since XORing the same value twice returns to the original
XOR with 2: result=6^2=4 -- this cancels the FIRST 2's effect too
Final result: 4 -- the only number that appeared once, since every OTHER pair canceled itself out

sections.text: This solves a problem in O(n) time and O(1) EXTRA space -- no HashSet needed (unlike the Hashing lesson's duplicate-detection approach), since XOR's self-canceling property does all the work. This only applies when EVERY number appears exactly twice EXCEPT one -- a specific problem shape, not a general-purpose duplicate finder.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing & (bitwise AND) with && (logical AND from Operators) -- they operate on entirely different things: individual bits vs whole boolean expressions.
Forgetting 1 << position creates a mask with only ONE bit set -- a common building block for isolating, setting, or clearing a specific bit.

Misapplying the XOR-single-number trick to a problem where MULTIPLE numbers appear an odd number of times -- it only works cleanly when exactly one number appears an odd number of times and all others appear an even number of times.
Assuming >> always behaves like dividing by 2 exactly -- for negative numbers, right shift rounds toward negative infinity, which can surprise beginners expecting truncation toward zero.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between & and &&?
questions.answer: & is the bitwise AND operator, comparing individual bits of two numbers. && is the logical AND operator (from Operators), combining two boolean expressions -- they operate on entirely different kinds of values.

questions.question: How do you check if a specific bit is set in a number?
questions.answer: Use (num & (1 << position)) != 0 -- shifting 1 to create a mask isolating just that bit position, then ANDing with num to check if that bit is 1.

questions.question: How can XOR find the single number in an array where every other number appears exactly twice?
questions.answer: XORing all elements together cancels out every pair (since x ^ x = 0), leaving only the number that appears once, since x ^ 0 = x. This achieves O(n) time and O(1) extra space, without needing a HashSet.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Bit manipulation works directly on a number's binary representation using &, |, ^, ~, <<, and >>. A mask created with 1 << position isolates, sets, or clears a specific bit via AND, OR, and AND-with-NOT respectively. XOR's self-canceling property (x ^ x = 0, x ^ 0 = x) enables the classic single-number trick: XORing every element in an array where all but one number appears exactly twice leaves only the unpaired number, solving the problem in O(n) time and O(1) extra space, without a HashSet.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: & isolates or checks a bit. | sets a bit. & ~ clears a bit. ^ toggles a bit, and cancels itself out when XORed with its own value twice -- 'XOR everything, survivors reveal themselves.'

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: How do you check if bit position p is set in a number num?
What two properties of XOR make the single-number trick work?
Does the XOR single-number trick work if more than one number appears an odd number of times?

sections.title: Single Number
sections.problemStatement: Given a non-empty array where every element appears twice except one, return the element that appears only once.
hints: a XOR a = 0, and XOR is commutative/associative -- so XOR-ing everything cancels all paired values, leaving the unpaired one.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: XOR every element together; duplicates cancel to 0, leaving only the single number.

sections.title: Number of 1 Bits
sections.problemStatement: Given an unsigned integer, return the number of '1' bits it has (Hamming weight).
hints: Brian Kernighan's trick: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit -- repeat and count until n becomes 0.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Check all 32 bit positions individually with (n >> i) & 1, summing the set ones.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Brian Kernighan's trick: n & (n-1) removes the lowest set bit each time. The loop runs exactly once per set bit, so it's faster when few bits are set.

sections.title: Counting Bits
sections.problemStatement: Given n, return an array ans where ans[i] is the number of 1 bits in the binary representation of i, for every i from 0 to n.
hints: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1) -- the bit count of i equals the bit count of i with its last bit dropped, plus that last bit itself.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Build the answer bottom-up using the relation dp[i] = dp[i/2] + (i's last bit), reusing previously computed smaller values instead of recomputing from scratch.

sections.title: Reverse Bits
sections.problemStatement: Reverse the bits of a given 32-bit unsigned integer.
hints: Build the result bit by bit: shift result left, OR in the lowest bit of n, then shift n right, 32 times.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Loop 32 times: shift the accumulating result left by 1, OR in n's current lowest bit, then shift n right by 1 to expose the next bit.

sections.title: Power of Two
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer n, return true if it is a power of two (1, 2, 4, 8, ...).
hints: A power of two has EXACTLY one set bit.
n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit -- if n was a power of two, the result is 0.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: n must be positive, and n & (n-1) must be 0 (meaning n had exactly one bit set, which becomes 0 once that single bit is cleared).

sections.title: Missing Number
sections.problemStatement: Given an array containing n distinct numbers from the range [0, n], return the one number missing from the range.

examples.explanation: Range [0,3] minus the array's 3 elements leaves 2 missing.

hints: XOR every index 0..n and every array value together -- every number that appears in both cancels out, leaving only the missing one.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: XOR all indices 0..n with all array values; the missing number is the only one left unpaired after everything else cancels.

sections.title: Sum of Two Integers
sections.problemStatement: Given two integers, calculate their sum WITHOUT using the + or - operators.
hints: XOR gives the sum ignoring carries (like binary addition without carry propagation).
AND, then shifted left by 1, gives the carry bits. Repeat: sum = a^b, carry = (a&b)<<1, until carry becomes 0.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Simulate binary addition: XOR gives digit-wise sum ignoring carry, AND-then-shift gives the carry to add in next. Repeat until there's no carry left.
